# Editorial sources for natural developer documentation generated by LLMs — Pass 2

## 1. Executive summary

**Top 5 sources after deep evaluation.**

1. **Google Developer Documentation Style Guide** — the strongest single base. Editorially mature, freely licensed (CC BY 4.0), explicitly written for developer documentation, with worked recommended/not-recommended examples on most pages.
2. **Microsoft Writing Style Guide** — best complement: a clear voice model ("warm and relaxed, crisp and clear, ready to lend a hand"), strong machine-translation/global-English guidance, and detailed bias-free communication rules.
3. **Wikipedia: Signs of AI writing** (WikiProject AI Cleanup, published 1 August 2025) — the only editorially-curated catalogue of LLM-specific tells written by experienced human editors of large-scale technical-adjacent prose. Should drive the "AI-tell avoidance" rule family.
4. **GitLab Documentation Style Guide and Vale rules** — best operational reference for how a real engineering org turns prose conventions into enforceable rules, plus a concrete dialect-handling pattern.
5. **Atlassian Design System content guidance** — the strongest narrow module for error-message and warning-message prose; deeper and more developer-oriented than Microsoft's coverage.

**Single best base?** No single source covers the target domain. Google is the right *base*; the rest provide rule families Google does not cover well (LLM tells, error messages, dialect, commit structure).

**Primary base recommendation:** synthesis with Google as the spine, Microsoft for voice/global-English, Wikipedia for AI-tell avoidance, Atlassian for error/log messages, Conventional Commits for commit structure, and GitLab for the dialect-handling pattern.

**British-first, dialect switch, or split?** One British-first skill with an explicit dialect switch. There is no actively-maintained, packaged British-English style guide of equivalent depth to Google or Microsoft. GOV.UK provides plain-English defaults; alphagov/gds-vale-styles (5 GitHub stars, no releases) is the closest BrE Vale codification but is too thin to be the base. The rules in Google/Microsoft are almost entirely dialect-neutral once spelling is set; therefore reuse the US developer-doc rules under British spelling defaults.

**Authoring vs editing.** One skill with sections for *authoring*, *editing/rewrite*, and a *final-pass checklist*. The immediate problem is editing LLM drafts, but the same rules also prevent bad output when used in authoring prompts; splitting into two skills duplicates the rule body.

**LLM vs linter.** Subjective, context-sensitive rules (tone, hedging, AI tells, page-topic self-description, sentence balance, error-message empathy) belong in SKILL.md. Mechanical rules (terminology, future-tense, "currently", "please", title-case-in-headings, British/US spelling, commit-message regex, code-formatting in prose) belong in Vale/textlint config. Inclusive-language substitution lists belong in Vale, *not* in the LLM prompt, because their false-positive rate in developer text is high.

## 2. Deep candidate evaluation

| # | Source | Disposition | Author | Dialect | Best use | Strongest contribution | Main weakness | Authority / adoption | Maintenance | Examples | False-positive risk | Portability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Google Developer Documentation Style Guide** (`developers.google.com/style`) | **Top-5 base** | Google editorial team (Jed Hartman et al.) | US (with explicit "global audience" guidance) | Authoring + editing | Voice and tone for developer docs; word list; sentence/paragraph craft; positive/negative example pairs | US-only; soft on em-dash policy; light on error-message detail | Vale `Google` package; textlint preset; adopted by Kubernetes, AMP, Dart, score-spec | Active (page-stamped 2025-04-02 to 2026-04-27 across sections) | Yes, on most rule pages | Low | Easy |
| 2 | **Microsoft Writing Style Guide** (`learn.microsoft.com/style-guide`) | **Top-5 secondary** | Microsoft editorial team | US (with global-English chapter) | Authoring + editing | "Top 10 tips", brand voice, bias-free communication, writing-for-machine-translation chapter, modifier-stack rule | Sometimes leans toward marketing/UX copy; light on commit/PR/error structure | Vale `Microsoft` package; PerfectIt integration; cited by GitLab as source for numbers rule | Active (last updated 2025-01-08; GitHub repo `MicrosoftDocs/microsoft-style-guide` regularly updated) | Yes | Low | Easy |
| 3 | **GitLab Documentation Style Guide + Vale rules** (`docs.gitlab.com/development/documentation/styleguide`) | **Top-5 secondary** | GitLab Technical Writing team | US-enforced (via `British.yml` substitution) | Linting + dialect pattern | Concrete Vale rule families (`FutureTense`, `CurrentStatus`, `SubstitutionWarning`, `SentenceLength`, `SelfReferential`), translation-friendly writing, dialect-switch pattern | Heavily GitLab-specific; uses "we" voice; some rules over-applied (passive, -ing) | Vale rules consumed by gitlab-runner and other GitLab-org projects; cited as reference in third-party tutorials | Very active (continuous-update model) | Partial | Medium | Medium |
| 4 | **Vale + errata-ai style packages** (`vale.sh`) | Supporting (engine + rule corpus) | Joseph Kato (`@jdkato`) and errata-ai org | Engine-neutral; packages mostly US | Linting layer | Standard delivery mechanism for Microsoft, Google, write-good, proselint, alex; Markdown-aware so code blocks are ignored | Engine only — rule quality is package-dependent | 5.1k stars on the live `vale-cli/vale` repo (formerly errata-ai/vale); adopted by GitLab, Homebrew, Linode, CockroachDB, Spotify | Active (releases through 2025–2026) | Via package fixtures | N/A (engine) | N/A |
| 5 | **Wikipedia: Signs of AI writing** (`en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Signs_of_AI_writing`) | **Top-5 secondary** | WikiProject AI Cleanup (Wikipedia community) | English-neutral | LLM-tell avoidance (authoring + editing) | Editorially curated catalogue of LLM-specific tells: em-dash overuse, "It's not X, it's Y" parallelisms, vague attributions, superficial -ing tail clauses, rigid "Challenges" sections, vertical-list-with-bold-inline-header pattern, conjunction overuse | Written for encyclopedic prose, not developer docs; some signs (curly quotes, emojis in headings) only partly transfer; the list itself warns it is a catalogue of *patterns*, not a ban list | Published 1 August 2025; widely cited in trade press (LitHub, FlowingData, Stack Overflow blog) and informs commercial style coverage | Active community editing (talk-page archive shows continuous revision through 2026) | Yes (the page itself shows AI-style and human-style diffs) | Low if applied as guidance, not as a regex blacklist | Medium |
| 6 | **proselint** (`github.com/amperser/proselint`) | Background / reject as direct LLM rule import | Suchow, Nytelife26 et al. | US literary defaults | Optional linting | Curated rules drawn from Garner, Pinker, Strunk & White, Orwell | Designed for literary, not developer, prose; the project's own 2016 SciPy paper reports a 1:10 false-positive-to-true-positive rate as a feature; not syntax-aware; `hyperbole.misc`, `lexical_illusions` and uncomparables rules have a long history of false positives (issues #971, #1192, errata-ai/proselint#1) | 4.5k GitHub stars on `amperser/proselint` (May 2026); still maintained | Hotfixes through 2025 | Yes (in test files) | High when used on Markdown with code | Hard |
| 7 | **GOV.UK Style Guide / GOV.UK content guidance** (`gov.uk/guidance/style-guide`) | Supporting (BrE plain-English source) | Government Digital Service | British | Authoring (dialect baseline) | Plain-English defaults; "words to avoid" list; reading-age target (~9); contraction guidance; concrete BrE A–Z | Government, not developer, audience; "no contractions" rule clashes with Google/Microsoft conversational tone; current top-level page is being replaced by "GOV.UK content and publishing guidance" | The original style across UK government; alphagov/gds-vale-styles codifies it as Vale rules | Page being migrated to a new home; A–Z still authoritative | Sparse but present | Low for spelling/words; medium for tone if applied literally to dev docs | Medium |
| 8 | **Kubernetes Documentation Style Guide** (`kubernetes.io/docs/contribute/style/style-guide`) | Narrow module (API/code-object naming) | Kubernetes SIG Docs | US | Authoring (narrow) | API-object naming and capitalisation rules (PascalCase, "a Secret object", angle-bracket placeholders); explicit "exception" overrides for active-voice and tense rules where awkward | Mostly redundant with Google; project-specific terminology | Used by Kubernetes ecosystem (Gateway API extends it) | Active | Yes | Low | Easy (narrow) |
| 9 | **Atlassian Design System content guidance** (`atlassian.design/content`) | **Top-5 secondary** (narrow module) | Atlassian Design team | US | Authoring + editing (error/warning/empty/feature messages) | Best detailed coverage of error-message structure: title constraints (3–4 words), reason+action+consequence pattern, "avoid please and sorry", CTA verb rules, distinction between error/warning/info/success/discovery | Product-UI focus rather than developer-CLI logs; some advice ("use we instead of you" in errors) contradicts Google/Microsoft for general docs | Public design system used by Jira/Confluence/Bitbucket ecosystem | Active | Yes | Low | Easy (as narrow module) |
| 10 | **Conventional Commits 1.0.0** (`conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/`) | **Top-5 secondary** (narrow module) | Conventional Commits working group | English-neutral | Authoring | Stable, RFC-2119-worded grammar for commit messages and `BREAKING CHANGE` footer; type/scope/description/body/footer structure | Defines structure, not natural-language quality of the description | Empirical study cited on Wikipedia: 360 of 381 sampled top-tier npm repositories contain commits that conform to Conventional Commits, and 198 projects have over 80% conforming commits | Spec stable at 1.0.0; site maintained | Yes | Very low | Easy |

## 3. What each top-5 source contributes

### Google Developer Documentation Style Guide
- **Rule families:** voice and tone for developers; sentence and paragraph craft; second person, present tense, active voice, serial comma; "things to avoid" list (please, simply, easy, let's, exclamation marks); link-text rules; placeholder and code-format conventions; "Write for a global audience" chapter.
- **Beyond baseline:** the baseline skill enforces structural punctuation rules (em-dash, hedging, gerunds), but does not encode a positive voice. Google provides the *target voice*: "a knowledgeable friend who understands what the developer wants to do".
- **Different from baseline:** Google is permissive about contractions and conversational tone, where the baseline is more austere. This is a *useful* contradiction: developer prose should be tighter than chatty marketing copy but warmer than government plain English.
- **Use as:** authoring guidance and editing guidance.

### Microsoft Writing Style Guide
- **Rule families:** "Top 10 tips" (short sentences, lead with the action, "talk like a person"); bias-free communication (gender-neutral, disability framing, avoid militaristic terms); writing for machine translation (avoid modifier stacks, idioms, ambiguous *-ing*, ambiguous *since/because*); numbers, capitalisation, procedure structure.
- **Beyond baseline:** explicit machine-translation/global-English chapter; modifier-stack rule; concrete inclusive-language rewrites that are less aggressive than alex.js.
- **Different from baseline:** Microsoft permits "it's OK to use a plural pronoun (they, their, or them) in generic references to a single person" — a useful clarification for the baseline's inclusive-language rule.
- **Use as:** authoring guidance.

### Wikipedia: Signs of AI writing
- **Rule families:** em-dash overuse; overuse of formulaic conjunctions ("moreover", "furthermore", "on the other hand"); negative parallelisms ("it's not X, it's Y", "not only X but also Y"); vague attributions ("has been described as"); superficial *-ing* tail clauses adding "significance"; rigid "Challenges and Future Prospects" sections; promotional/press-release tone; vertical lists with bold inline header + colon (relevant to developer docs that legitimately use this pattern).
- **Beyond baseline:** the baseline covers em-dash and hedging mechanically; Wikipedia adds a positive *catalogue* of the higher-level patterns ("it's not X, it's Y", -ing tail clauses, conjunction overuse, vague attribution) that the baseline does not name.
- **Useful contradiction with baseline:** Wikipedia explicitly says em-dash overuse is "most useful when taken in combination with other indicators, not by itself". This argues against the baseline's *strict* em-dash ban — the right behaviour is to lower frequency, not to forbid.
- **Use as:** editing guidance (a checklist of tells to scan for), not as a regex blacklist.

### GitLab Documentation Style Guide and Vale rules
- **Rule families:** named, implementable rules — `FutureTense`, `CurrentStatus`, `SubstitutionWarning`, `SentenceLength` (max 25 words), `SelfReferential` ("This guide explains…"), `Acronyms`, `ReferenceLinks`, `MultiLineLinks`; "avoid `allow`/`enable`" customer-perspective rule; translation-friendly checklist (avoid *-ing*, ambiguous *since*, Latin abbreviations, idioms).
- **Beyond baseline:** the baseline forbids page-topic self-description; GitLab's `SelfReferential.yml` is the only widely-deployed implementation of this rule and validates the rule's existence. GitLab also offers the dialect-switch pattern (a single `British.yml` substitution file enforces the chosen dialect — GitLab's choice is US, but the same mechanism can run in reverse).
- **Use as:** linting guidance (Vale config) plus dialect handling.

### Atlassian Design System content guidance
- **Rule families:** error-message structure (informative scannable title in sentence case; reason + action + consequence; second-sentence backup solution); avoid "please" and "sorry" in errors; CTA verbs ("Save", "Remove") not "OK"; distinct guidance for error vs warning vs info vs success vs feature-discovery; one to two-word CTAs.
- **Beyond baseline:** the baseline says nothing specific about error messages. Atlassian provides a complete sub-skill for failure-state prose.
- **Different from baseline:** Atlassian recommends "we" rather than "you" in error messages — useful as a *narrow* override to the general "second person" rule, restricted to error/failure states.
- **Use as:** authoring + editing guidance for an error-message module.

## 4. Worked examples

### Google
- *Recommended:* "To view the document, click View." *Not recommended:* "To view the document, please click View." — Teaches that politeness fillers add noise without helping the reader.
- *Avoid:* "wackiness, zaniness… phrasing in terms of let's do something… using phrases like simply, It's that simple, It's easy, or quickly in a procedure." — Teaches the LLM-prose problem of false friendliness, which is a known AI tell.

### Microsoft
- *Recommended:* "Your migration will proceed more smoothly if you have a project plan that's well thought out." *Not recommended:* "With an extremely well thought-out Windows migration project plan, your migration will go more smoothly." — Teaches modifier-stack avoidance, directly applicable to LLM drafts that pile adjectives.
- *Active voice:* "Click the button to start the process," not "The button can be clicked to start the process." — Teaches the baseline active-voice rule with the cognitive-load justification.

### Wikipedia: Signs of AI writing
- The page contrasts an AI-written passage on a Catalan statistical body, containing phrases like *"represented a significant shift toward regional statistical independence, enabling Catalonia to develop a statistical system tailored to its unique socio-…"*, against a terser human revision. Teaches the *-ing* tail-clause and undue-significance tells in one short passage.
- The em-dash section gives the verbatim guidance: LLMs *"use them more often than nonprofessional human-written text of the same genre, and use them in places where humans are more likely to use commas, parentheses, colons, or (misused) hyphens"*. Teaches the rule-of-thumb rather than a ban.

### GitLab
- *Recommended:* "Merge requests let you compare code." *Not recommended:* "GitLab allows you to compare code." — Teaches the customer-perspective rule and the avoid-`allow`/`enable` rule together.
- Vale output example: `gitlab.FutureTense "will be"` and `gitlab.CurrentStatus "Currently"`. Teaches that mechanically-detectable hedges and future-tense weasel words are exactly the rules that belong in a linter, not the LLM prompt.

### Atlassian
- For error titles: *"Limit titles to three to four words where possible, excluding 'an', 'a', or 'the'."* Teaches the title constraint that the baseline skill lacks.
- *"Avoid using 'please' and 'sorry'. Saying 'sorry' in error messages can make the situation worse by causing errors to appear more severe than they actually are."* Teaches the empathy-without-apology pattern.

## 5. Pitfalls and false positives

- **proselint**: the project's own SciPy 2016 paper frames a 1:10 false-positive-to-true-positive rate as best-in-class — meaning a 10% false-positive rate is *acceptable* by design. Tracker examples: `hyperbole.misc` false-positives on Markdown code (`#971`); `lexical_illusions.misc` hotfix in 2025 (`#1192`); `uncomparables` flagging "every perfect instance" as "very perfect" (PR #1152). The errata-ai port has had recurring issues with the Material for MkDocs admonition syntax (`!!!`). **Conclusion: do not import proselint rules wholesale into the LLM skill.**
- **write-good**: passive-voice and "weasel" rules are too crude for technical prose. The Microsoft style guide and the UNC writing centre both note that scientific and procedural English legitimately uses passive voice ("Use passive voice if active voice leads to an awkward construction" — Kubernetes style guide). **Conclusion: keep passive-voice detection as a Vale suggestion, not an LLM rule.**
- **alex.js**: well-documented false-positive problem in technical text. Suzanne Aitchison's DEV.to writeup *"Refactoring condescending language with alexjs"* (25 April 2020) reports running alex over a documentation site and getting *"104 warnings… Usage of 'easy'/'easily', 'simple'/'simply' and 'just' in my tutorials… False positives for use of words like 'color' and 'special'"*; common mitigations include `profanitySureness: 1` and per-rule allowlists (`hostesses-hosts` for *network hosts*, `execute`, `failed` for testing tools). **Conclusion: inclusive-language substitutions belong in Vale with project-level allow lists, not in the LLM rule body.**
- **Readability-score rules** (Flesch–Kincaid in the Vale `Readability` package): noisy on prose containing code identifiers; not directly applicable to docstrings or commit subject lines. **Conclusion: exclude from skill.**
- **GitLab `FutureTense` and `CurrentStatus`**: occasional false positives where "will" is a legitimate semantic modal ("the build will fail if X is missing"). The GitLab project addresses this by setting the rule at warning level. **Conclusion: implement as Vale suggestion, with the LLM rule phrased as "prefer present tense; use *will* only when the future is the actual subject".**
- **Wikipedia: Signs of AI writing**: the page warns *"This list is not a ban on certain words, phrases, or punctuation. … Not all text featuring the following indicators is AI-generated."* Talk-page discussions have already moved "section summaries" and the conjunction-overuse rule toward lower confidence. **Conclusion: apply as a frequency-balancing checklist, not as forbidden tokens.**
- **GOV.UK "no contractions" rule**: contradicts Google and Microsoft. GOV.UK forbids contractions because of literacy considerations for the general public; Google explicitly endorses them ("contracted verb forms… are perfectly acceptable"). For developer docs the Google position wins. **Conclusion: do not import GOV.UK's contraction rule.**
- **Strict em-dash avoidance (baseline)**: Wikipedia frames em-dash overuse as a *frequency* and *placement* problem ("formulaic, pat way… over-emphasising clauses or parallelisms"), not as a forbidden character. The arXiv preprint *"The Last Fingerprint: How Markdown Training Shapes LLM Prose"* (Freeburg, arXiv:2603.27006, March 2026) reports a two-condition suppression experiment across twelve models from five providers and finds em-dash frequency varying from 0.0 per 1,000 words (Llama) up to 9.1 (GPT-4.1 under suppression), arguing that em-dash frequency is a structural artefact of markdown-saturated training data. **Conclusion: the baseline's strict ban is defensible as a counter-frequency lever; soften only the rationale in the skill, not the rule.**

## 6. British-English strategy

- **Primary BrE source:** **GOV.UK A–Z and "words to avoid"** for spelling, vocabulary and plain-English defaults. Use as the dictionary and "avoid this word, prefer that" baseline.
- **Should GOV.UK be the primary British source?** Only for spelling/word choices. For developer voice, GOV.UK is too austere (no contractions, no -tion gerunds, reading-age-9 target). Layer Google's voice rules over GOV.UK's spelling.
- **US developer-doc rules reusable under British spelling:** essentially all of Google and Microsoft's tone, voice, sentence-structure, list-parallelism, link-text, code-formatting, modifier-stack, and global-English rules. They are dialect-neutral once spelling is set.
- **Dialect-specific variants needed:** spelling (-ise/-ize, colour/color, organisation/organization); the Oxford serial comma is recommended by both Google and Microsoft and is also acceptable in British English (it is the Oxford University Press house style), so retain it; date format (DD Month YYYY in BrE vs Month DD, YYYY in US); single vs double quotation marks (BrE prefers single, US double).
- **British-English Vale/textlint package status:** there is no actively-maintained, packaged BrE *style* guide in the Vale or textlint ecosystem. **alphagov/gds-vale-styles** (5 GitHub stars, 12 commits, no releases, ~3 contributors) is the only codification of a true BrE style guide for Vale; its description is *"The GDS content style guide codified as Vale config files"*. Everything else British in the Vale ecosystem is either (a) a US-spelling substitution list run in reverse (GitLab's `British.yml`, Alec Clews's inverted variant) or (b) a Hunspell `en_GB` dictionary swap. The textlint ecosystem has no `textlint-rule-preset-british` equivalent.
- **Recommended approach:** package the skill BrE-first, with a `dialect: en-GB | en-US` switch that toggles (i) the spelling Hunspell dictionary, (ii) substitution lists, (iii) date/quotation conventions. The skill's prose rules remain identical across dialects.
- **Repos already in US English:** detect dialect from existing prose (Google-style words, US spelling) and yield to it. The baseline skill already does dialect-drift detection; extend it so that if more than ~5% of a sample is US-spelled, default to US even though the skill itself is BrE-first.

## 7. Authoring vs editing strategy

Recommend **one skill, three internal sections**:

1. **Authoring guidance** — voice, tone, structure, what to write first, how to write a procedure, how to write a docstring, how to write an error message, how to write a commit message. ~40% of the skill.
2. **Editing/rewrite guidance** — what to look for in an LLM draft and how to rewrite it: em-dash density, hedging stacks, *-ing* tail clauses, "it's not X, it's Y" parallelisms, vague attributions, page-topic self-description, modifier stacks, future-tense, "currently", undue-significance closers. ~40%.
3. **Final-pass checklist** — short, scannable: dialect consistency, code-format, commit-message format, error-message format, AI tells. ~20%.

The editing section is the immediate priority because the trigger problem is LLM-drafted prose. The authoring section prevents the same problems when the LLM is asked to draft from scratch. Splitting into two separate skills would duplicate ~60% of the rule body and would force the maintainer to choose one at invocation time, which mis-models the actual workflow (draft → edit → final pass usually happens within a single task).

## 8. LLM skill vs linter split

**SKILL.md (LLM guidance):**
- Em-dash overuse and placement (frequency + placement, not ban).
- Weak hedging stacks (more than one hedge per sentence).
- Long mechanically balanced sentences; split into clear units.
- AI-tell avoidance: -ing tail clauses, "it's not X, it's Y", "moreover/furthermore" overuse, undue-significance closers, "Challenges and Future Prospects" rigidity, vague attributions, promotional tone.
- Page-topic self-description.
- Gerund vs infinitive after *rather than* / *instead of*.
- Error-message structure (Atlassian pattern).
- Voice and tone (Google's "knowledgeable friend").
- When to break the rule (Google's Orwell-quoting caveat).
- Dialect-drift detection.

**Vale/textlint/proselint-style automated checks:**
- Dialect consistency (`British.yml`/`American.yml` substitutions; Hunspell `en_GB`/`en_US`).
- Future-tense and "currently" hedges (`gitlab.FutureTense`, `gitlab.CurrentStatus`).
- "Please" and "sorry" in error messages.
- Terminology word lists (per-project).
- Code-in-text formatting (backticks for inline code, sentence-case headings, list parallelism).
- Commit-message grammar (Conventional Commits + commitlint).
- Modifier-stack length (regex on long noun phrases).
- Sentence length (max 25–30 words as a warning).
- Inclusive-language substitutions (with per-project allow lists; *not* in the LLM rule body).
- Self-referential page openers (GitLab `SelfReferential.yml`).

**Optional human-review checklist:**
- Does the page open with the answer (BLUF)?
- Does each procedure step describe a discrete action?
- Are link texts front-loaded with the search term?
- Is the error message reason + action + consequence?
- Does the commit message describe *why*, not just *what*?

## 9. Recommended synthesis

**Primary base:** Google Developer Documentation Style Guide.
**Secondary:** Microsoft Writing Style Guide (voice, global English, bias-free), Wikipedia: Signs of AI writing (LLM tells), Atlassian (error messages), Conventional Commits (commit structure), GitLab (Vale rule patterns and dialect switch).
**Background-only:** GOV.UK (BrE spelling/words), Kubernetes (API-object naming), alphagov/gds-vale-styles (BrE codification reference), Vale engine.
**Sources to avoid as direct rule imports:** proselint (literary, syntax-unaware, high false-positive rate by its own admission), write-good (crude passive/weasel rules), alex.js (high false-positive rate in technical text; use only behind allow lists).

**Proposed skill shape (~1,400–1,800 words):**

- **Frontmatter** — name, scope, dialect default (en-GB), invocation rules.
- **§1 Voice and tone** — the Google "knowledgeable friend" target; the baseline strictness layered on top.
- **§2 Sentence and paragraph craft** — Microsoft's short sentences, lead with the action, modifier-stack limit; baseline's split-long-balanced-sentences rule.
- **§3 Punctuation and structure** — em-dash policy with the Wikipedia rationale; hyphen stacking; serial comma; sentence-case headings; list parallelism.
- **§4 AI-tell avoidance** — direct port of relevant categories from Wikipedia: Signs of AI writing.
- **§5 Hedging, certainty and self-description** — baseline rules; one hedge per sentence; no page-topic self-description.
- **§6 Inclusive language** — Microsoft's lighter-touch approach (rewrite to plural or to role) over alex.js's substitution list.
- **§7 Dialect** — en-GB default; switch and detection.
- **§8 Domain modules** — (a) docstrings/Javadoc/KDoc/TSDoc; (b) commit messages (Conventional Commits + imperative-mood summary, ≤ 72 chars, body explains *why*); (c) error and log messages (Atlassian pattern adapted for developer logs); (d) PR descriptions and review replies; (e) changelog/release-note entries.
- **§9 Final-pass checklist.**
- **§10 When to break the rule** — Orwell-via-Google.

Include both **positive and negative examples** (the Google "Recommended / Not recommended" format). Keep each rule to ≤ 3 lines plus one short example pair. Avoid long quoted passages.

**Companion Vale/textlint config:** yes — ship a thin `.vale.ini` template that loads `Google`, `Microsoft` and a small project-local style pack containing GitLab-derived `FutureTense`, `CurrentStatus`, `SelfReferential`, `SubstitutionWarning` plus a configurable `British.yml` substitution table. Recommend `alex` only with `profanitySureness: 1` and a starter allowlist for technical terms.

**10–15 rule families to port into the skill:**

1. **Voice — knowledgeable-friend tone** (Google).
2. **Lead with the action; one idea per sentence; ≤ ~25 words** (Microsoft, GitLab).
3. **Active voice with explicit exceptions for awkward subjects** (Kubernetes override of Google).
4. **Modifier-stack limit; avoid noun pile-ups** (Microsoft).
5. **No page-topic self-description; no "This guide explains…"** (GitLab `SelfReferential`).
6. **Em-dash frequency control, not ban; never as a paragraph connector or definition-list separator** (baseline + Wikipedia rationale).
7. **AI-tell avoidance: -ing tail clauses, "it's not X, it's Y", "moreover/furthermore" overuse, undue-significance closers, vague attributions, promotional tone** (Wikipedia).
8. **One hedge per sentence; prefer present tense; avoid "currently" and bare "will"** (baseline + GitLab `FutureTense`, `CurrentStatus`).
9. **Inclusive language by rewrite, not substitution** (Microsoft over alex).
10. **List parallelism, sentence-case headings, front-loaded link text** (Google, GitLab).
11. **Error and warning messages: short title, reason + action + consequence, no "please/sorry", verb CTAs** (Atlassian).
12. **Commit messages: Conventional Commits structure, imperative present-tense summary ≤ ~72 chars, body explains *why*** (Conventional Commits + Angular convention).
13. **API and code-object naming: backticks for inline code, PascalCase preserved, angle brackets for placeholders** (Kubernetes + Google).
14. **Dialect: en-GB default with detection-driven switch; Oxford serial comma retained in both dialects** (synthesis).
15. **Final-pass checklist: BLUF; one idea per paragraph; dialect; code-format; commit format; error format; AI tells** (synthesis).

## Caveats

- Wikipedia: Signs of AI writing is a community-edited essay published 1 August 2025, not a peer-reviewed source; its talk-page archive shows active disagreement on the weight of individual signs (e.g. conjunction overuse). Apply as guidance, not as a regex blacklist.
- The proselint false-positive rate cited (1:10) is from the project's own 2016 SciPy paper; the project frames it as best-in-class for prose linters. Treat it as a ceiling, not a floor.
- The arXiv preprint *"The Last Fingerprint: How Markdown Training Shapes LLM Prose"* (Freeburg, arXiv:2603.27006, March 2026) is not peer-reviewed; it is cited only as a hypothesis for *why* em-dash frequency is an LLM signal, and as quantitative context (em-dash rates of 0.0–9.1 per 1,000 words across twelve models).
- GOV.UK's primary style page is being migrated to a new "GOV.UK content and publishing guidance" home; URLs may shift but the A–Z and "words to avoid" content remain authoritative.
- alphagov/gds-vale-styles has 5 GitHub stars, 12 commits, no releases — it codifies the right source but cannot be relied on as a maintained dependency; treat as a reference, not as an upstream.
- Atlassian's "use 'we' instead of 'you'" advice for error messages directly contradicts the general second-person rule from Google and Microsoft. Scope the override to failure-state prose only.
